Its construction was ordered in 1560 by**Cosimo I de’ Medici**, known as Cosimo the Great and first Grand Duke of Tuscany, to house the administrative and judiciary offices of Florence, the “uffizi” (which in Italian means “offices”). At the time when the grandiose building was being built, the Medici hegemony was secure over Florence. Vasari Corridor The nearby Fabbrica degli Uffizi represents one of the great innovations brought to Florence by Cosimo’s favourite architect, Giorgio Vasari. The construction of the Uffizi will offer the opportunity to redesign a vast portion of the urban fabric between Palazzo Vecchio and the river Arno. The intermediate space between the two parallel arms of the new building has a dual function: on the one hand, it serves as a street, on the other as a square. This was the seat of the magistracy(Uffizi) now totally subjugated, even spatially, to Cosimo’s power.
